title: Collective emission of subwavelengths atom-like emitter arrays in the presence of inhomogeneous broadening
authors: Uri Israeli, Shahar Levi, Sagi Ben-Avi, Ada Kransnovsky, Daniel Silvian, Shlomo Winberg, Rivka Bekenstein
abstract: Quantum metasurfaces comprised of subwavelength atomic arrays emerged as a promising platform for enhanced atom-photon interaction. However, realizing such a system with solid-state emitters has been considered impractical due to strong inhomogeneous broadening, which was expected to suppress the photon-mediated interactions that underpin collective emission. Here we report the observation of collective emission from subwavelength arrays of silicon-vacancy centres in diamond -- solid-state emitters whose inhomogeneous broadening exceeds the natural linewidth by two orders of magnitude -- demonstrating that collective effects such as resonance shifts, modified decay rates and directional coherent emission survive this disorder. A crucial enabling element is the implantation of a high density of silicon ions at each array site. This creates so-called superatoms, local ensembles that probabilistically achieve frequency matching across the array and enhance the collective response. We support our observations   with a theoretical analysis explaining the mechanisms that preserve the collective effects even in the presence of inhomogeneity. These observations have direct implications for the realization of subwavelength arrays in any solid-state system, paving the way for quantum-emitter metasurfaces that are naturally integrated into nanophotonic environments.
summary: The authors report observing robust collective emission from arrays of silicon-vacancy centers in diamond, overcoming the theoretical hurdle posed by strong inhomogeneous broadening. By engineering 'superatoms' at each site, they demonstrate that solid-state emitter arrays can function as highly controllable quantum-emitter metasurfaces. This paves the way for integrating quantum light sources directly into nanophotonic circuits.
main_problem: The primary challenge addressed is demonstrating collective emission from subwavelength emitter arrays despite strong inhomogeneous broadening, a factor previously thought to suppress photon-mediated interactions in solid-state systems.
main_result: Collective emission was successfully observed, showing characteristic resonance shifts and decay rates that confirm the array acts as a functional quantum-emitter metasurface. This proves that collective effects survive significant disorder in solid-state emitters.
method: The work combines experimental measurements of directional emission spectra (momentum mapping) with theoretical analysis using effective Hamiltonians and simulations to model superradiance probability.
model_or_system: The system utilizes subwavelength arrays of silicon-vacancy (SiV) centers in diamond, which are engineered into 'superatoms' via high-density ion implantation. The physics is modeled by diagonalizing an effective Hamiltonian describing collective dipole interactions within the array structure.
key_observables: Collective resonance frequency shift ($\Delta$), photonic band $\omega(k_{\perp})$, directional emission intensity mapped vs. transverse momentum ($k_x, k_y$), and superradiance probability.
important_parameters: The natural linewidth of SiV centers is much smaller than the inhomogeneous broadening (tens of GHz); collective effects are observed when the effective linewidth ($\Gamma_{	ext{eff}}$) reaches specific thresholds.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations like Born–Markov, and the initial success hinges on the 'superatom' concept created by ion implantation to overcome frequency mismatch.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spectral dependence on momentum (Fourier space), showing characteristic dispersion relations for collective modes; other figures map emission intensity vs. momentum and show spatial mode distributions.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem of inhomogeneity, presents experimental measurements confirming collective shifts and band structures across multiple SiV transitions, supports these findings with theoretical modeling of superradiance, and concludes by demonstrating the viability of this platform for integrated quantum photonics.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses how to engineer coherent quantum light sources using solid-state defects in nanophotonic environments. The ability to maintain collective coherence despite large disorder is a major breakthrough for realizing scalable quantum emitters.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 a. Experimental setup for probing the collective emission of atom-like emitter arrays. b. The SiV centre is an atom-like emitter system whose ground and excited states are localized at the diamond bandgap. Its spin and optical degeneracies are partially lifted by the spin–orbit interaction, which splits each quartet into two degenerate doublets, so that there are four dipole-allowed optical transitions. These are resonant with light around 737 nm (marked A,B,C,D). We are interested in probing the collective resonance with decay rate Γcollective and spectral shift ∆relative to the bare SiV resonance. c. Each array site is comprised of tens of SiV centres with spectral inhomogeneity...

Fig. 2 a. Spectrum of the collective emission of a SiV subwavelength array, with the four SiV optical transitions as detected in the direction normal to the arrays as a function of the momentum parallel to the array. b,d. The collective linewidth and shifts of the specific collective states emitting as a function of momentum, extracted from the fit for the D transition. c. Cross sections of the spectrum for different momenta, showing the shift in emission frequency, marked (i),(ii),(iii) in all subplots (Inset highlights frequency shift).

Fig. 3 Directional emission of the collective response of the array. a. Intensity distribution detected in the direction normal to the array as a function of the momentum parallel to the array. b. Cross sections of the intensity as a function of kx for different momenta, showing the shift in emission frequency. Red-shaded parts mark high-intensity areas. c. Correlations between the extracted group velocity in e and the intensity in b. d. Schematics of the measurement setup, where a lens maps the emission into momentum space and imaging of the lens back focal plane reveals the intensity distribution in k-space. e. Fig. 4 a. Measured spatial distribution of the asymmetric collective mode for a square array in the far-field. b. Anisotropic far-field emission of a dipole near the diamond–air interface as calculated by FDTD and compared to the measured uncoupled dipole emission (blue line). c. Schematics of a dipole orientated in the (1,1,1) direction and placed 60 nm beneath the diamond–air surface (displayed is the calculated electric field perpendicular to the array plane). d,e. Calculated asymmetric subradiant modes of square and honeycomb arrays. f. Partially Pumped Collective Emitters.note: The observation of collective emission from an array of emitters, especially when considering disorder (inhomogeneous broadening) which acts as a form of local variation or 'partial' suppression/modification of ideal coupling, directly relates to the concept of controlling superradiance in structured arrays.
